**Limes** are sour green citrus fruits grown in tropical and subtropical regions.**[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)**

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Limes have the characteristic traits of other citrus fruits, including their dimpled skin and juicy flesh. Most limes are picked and sold when dark- to yellow-green in color,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) though they actually fully ripen to a yellow-orange color on the tree.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) They do not ripen further once picked, staying green. The pulp is a pale yellow-green color, and the juice of most varieties is quite acidic with little sugar.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) The characteristic lime aroma comes from various terpene compounds, such as (+)-limonene.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

The **Key lime**, also called **Mexican lime**, **West Indian lime**, or "true lime" is *Citrus aurantifolia*.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) These limes tend to be smaller, rounder, seedier, more aromatic, and slightly more yellow in color.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Their flavor tends to be influenced by their growing conditions, with those grown in Mexico tending to have a pronounced bitterness and astringency.[[13]](#cite_note-:11-13) The **Persian lime**, also known as the **Tahitian lime** or **Bearss lime**, is *C. latifolia*.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) It is common in North America and Europe,[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) and it features a large oblong shape, dark green skin, and bright flavor.[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[13]](#cite_note-:11-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Other minor lime varieties include the **mandarin/Rangpur lime** (*C. limonia*), **sweet/limetta lime** (*C. limetta)*, and **Palestinian/Indian sweet lime** (*C. limettoides).*[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

## Seasonality

[edit | edit source]

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Like with other citrus fruits, look for limes that are firm, shiny, and heavy,[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) without significant blemishes, bruising, dryness, or soft spots.[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) Limes with more yellow coloration tend to be less tart than greener limes.[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) Store whole limes in plastic in the fridge, where they will keep for up to several weeks.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Though generally too tart and bitter to consume raw and on their own,[[15]](#cite_note-:6-15) limes are used in a range of cuisines globally, especially as a seasoning. The juice is used for flavor and acidity in various beverages, desserts, confections, condiments, marinades, and more.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:6-15) The zest can be bitter, but it also contributes flavor and color.[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:6-15) In western Asia, the whole fruits may be boiled and dried to produce a seasoning used in savory soups and stews.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

In some cases limes and lemons can be substituted for each other;[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[15]](#cite_note-:6-15) however, note that the specific aromatic profiles are quite different. The colors may also differ, which can be relevant when using as a garnish.
